frameworks.name: discrete-slot working memory model
frameworks.one_line: Traditional view of CDA as pure index of item load in fixed slots

frameworks.name: continuous resource working memory
frameworks.one_line: Flexible allocation of a graded resource across memoranda

frameworks.name: hierarchical working memory systems
frameworks.one_line: Sensory delay activity reflects multiple layered processes beyond storage

frameworks.name: attentional and executive control
frameworks.one_line: CDA also indexes attentional selection and executive operations

core_question: Does sustained contralateral delay activity reflect only memory item load, or also attentional, executive, and perceptual mechanisms?
key_assumptions: Sustained contralateral delay activity can be measured in visual and tactile modalities
CDA amplitude can reflect more than one underlying cognitive operation
Visual and tactile WM share comparable electrophysiological signatures
manipulations: Unisensory vs multisensory visual and tactile working memory tasks
Tasks probing storage vs attention vs executive operations
Varying cognitive operations during the retention period
key_findings: Sensory delay activity is sensitive to cognitive operations beyond discrete item storage
CDA reflects attentional and executive processes in addition to load
Components are consistent with flexible continuous resource allocation rather than discrete slots
Visual and tactile delay activity show analogous sensitivity across operations
interpretations: CDA is not a pure measure of item load but indexes hierarchically organized WM systems
Sensory delay activity reflects executive, attentional, and perceptual mechanisms jointly with storage
